metadata.title: Plan Approval Gate: The 5-Question Review Checklist

beats.title: Liam cold open — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: I want to try running that 5-question gate against a plan of my own instead of watching one clear on the second pass. I'm most shaky on question five, since 'when done' slipped through as non-binary until it got rewritten as 'output.md exists and wc -l under 100 returns true.' Take a plan I'll paste and (1) score each of the five questions pass or fail with one-sentence reasoning, (2) rewrite every failing item into a specific revision note the way Q5 was fixed, and (3) give the final verdict of approve, revise, or reject.

props.command: I want to try running that 5-question gate against a plan of my own instead of watching one clear on the second pass. I'm most shaky on question five, since 'when done' slipped through as non-binary until it got rewritten as 'output.md exists and wc -l under 100 returns true.' Take a plan I'll paste and (1) score each of the five questions pass or fail with one-sentence reasoning, (2) rewrite every failing item into a specific revision note the way Q5 was fixed, and (3) give the final verdict of approve, revise, or reject.

beats.narration_text: Five questions. If any one fails, the plan doesn't execute. That's the gate.

The approval gate exists because plans fail in predictable ways: scope too broad, sources inappropriate, exclusions vague, output artifact unclear, stop conditions untestable. The 5-question checklist catches all five before execution. Approving without review is abdication — it looks like oversight and provides none of it.

Review this proposed plan against 5 questions: Is scope correctly bounded? Are allowed sources appropriate? Are exclusions specific enough? Is output artifact clearly defined? Are stop conditions binary and testable? For each: pass or fail with one-sentence reasoning. Final verdict: approve, revise, or reject.

props.command: claude "Review this proposed plan against these 5 questions: (1) Is scope correctly bounded? (2) Are allowed sources appropriate? (3) Are exclusions specific enough? (4) Is output artifact clearly defined? (5) Are stop conditions binary and testable? For each: pass/fail with one-sentence reasoning. Final verdict: approve, revise, or reject." < plan.md

beats.narration_text: The plan gate script runs 5 questions, produces pass or fail per question, and gives a final verdict with revision notes for each failure.

props.code:
QUESTIONS = [
    ("Q1", "Is scope correctly bounded?",
     lambda p: p.get("scope") and "all" not in p["scope"].lower()),
    ("Q2", "Are allowed sources appropriate?",
     lambda p: bool(p.get("allowed_sources"))),
    ("Q3", "Are exclusions specific enough?",
     lambda p: p.get("exclusions") and len(p["exclusions"]) > 10),
    ("Q4", "Is output artifact clearly defined?",
     lambda p: p.get("output_artifact") and "." in p["output_artifact"]),
    ("Q5", "Are stop conditions binary and testable?",
     lambda p: p.get("stop_conditions") and "when done" not in p["stop_conditions"].lower()),
]

def run_gate(plan: dict) -> dict:
    results = []
    for qid, question, test in QUESTIONS:
        passed = test(plan)
        results.append({"id": qid, "question": question, "verdict": "PASS" if passed else "FAIL"})
    all_pass = all(r["verdict"] == "PASS" for r in results)
    final = "APPROVE" if all_pass else "REVISE"
    return {"results": results, "final_verdict": final}

beats.narration_text: Plan reviewed: Q1 PASS — scope bounded to Q3 operations. Q2 PASS — sources internal only. Q3 PASS — exclusions specific. Q4 PASS — output: single markdown file under 2 pages. Q5 FAIL — stop condition: when done is not binary. Revision note: replace with when output.md exists and wc dash-l under 100 returns true. Final verdict: REVISE.
Revise the stop condition to be binary and testable; re-run approval gate.

Revised plan: Q5 now PASS — when output.md exists and wc dash-l under 100 returns true is binary and testable without human interpretation. All 5 questions: PASS. Final verdict: APPROVE. The gate cleared on the second pass because the revision was specific enough to eliminate the ambiguity.

The 5-question gate is minimum viable oversight. It does not guarantee the plan is good — it guarantees the plan is reviewable. A plan that fails Q5 hasn't specified what done means. Approving it means the human is delegating the definition of done to the AI. The gate prevents that.
That's the full CoWork series — ten tools for making AI delegation accountable.
Nik Bear Brown. Brutalist and Educational AI. nikbearbrown.com.
